#アクセサリー

髪: 緑の髪の片側に、小さな押し花（忘れな草とシダ）を使ったヘアクリップ。

#アクション
片手でお玉を持っており、猛威法の手で小皿に注いだ出汁を口元へ運ぼうとしている

6等身

The case centers on Ori Solomon, a 55-year-old Israeli citizen and property manager who was in the United States on a non-immigrant visa. Authorities reportedly began investigating after a cleaner working at one of Solomon’s Las Vegas properties became severely ill upon entering a garage where suspicious scientific materials were found, including glass beakers with unknown liquids and equipment resembling laboratory devices. Media reports cited in the article also reference concerns that materials discovered could be consistent with biological substances, including potential pathogens, though these claims remain part of ongoing reporting rather than adjudicated findings.

Solomon was initially charged federally with firearm possession, while state-level authorities pursued allegations related to improper disposal of hazardous waste. However, the federal weapons charge was later dropped by the office of U.S. Attorney Sigal Chattah. The article highlights controversy surrounding Chattah’s appointment and her political views, which critics cited in Zeteo’s reporting as potentially influencing prosecutorial decisions. The report further connects the situation to broader political and geopolitical tensions, alleging a wider pattern of influence and emphasizing concerns raised by commentators about the integrity of legal decision-making.

The piece also notes additional background claims regarding related investigations involving other properties and individuals, as well as criticism from advocacy and media sources about political influence in federal legal processes. Supporters of the officials involved are not extensively represented in the article. Overall, the story frames the dismissal of charges as part of a broader debate about political influence, foreign nationals, and prosecutorial discretion within the U.S. justice system.
external.title: Trump linked to dismissal of Nevada illegal laboratory-related charges involving foreign national case

🤖 IA: It's clickbait ⚠️
👥 Users: It's clickbait ⚠️

external.description:
CBS News employees reportedly reacted with shock and anger after major leadership changes were announced at the long-running television news program '60 Minutes.' According to the article, CBS News head Bari Weiss removed executive producer Tanya Simon and senior executive producer Draggan Mihailovich from their positions despite the program experiencing strong ratings growth during the season. Staff members described the atmosphere inside the newsroom as emotional and deeply unsettled, with several employees allegedly comparing the situation to a funeral.

The report states that Nick Bilton, a technology journalist with limited television management experience, was selected as the new executive producer. The decision reportedly surprised many employees who believed the outgoing leadership team had performed successfully. Internal criticism focused on concerns that CBS executives were dismantling the culture and editorial stability of one of television's most respected newsmagazine programs.

Several unnamed producers and staffers quoted in the article expressed frustration and disappointment with network management. Some feared the changes could damage the future identity and functioning of '60 Minutes.' The article also noted uncertainty surrounding the future of veteran correspondent Lesley Stahl and other senior staff members whose contracts are nearing expiration.

Former '60 Minutes' correspondent Steve Kroft also commented on the situation, describing the developments as a dramatic turning point for the historic program. Overall, the article portrays the leadership overhaul as a controversial move that has created significant internal tension within CBS News.
external.title: CBS News Leadership Shake-Up Sparks Internal Backlash at '60 Minutes'

Fox News Host Acknowledges Reports of Trump Dozing in Meetings While Defending His Cognitive Health

external.description:
A Fox News segment sparked attention after host Brian Kilmeade appeared to acknowledge that President Donald Trump has been observed appearing to fall asleep during meetings, while simultaneously urging viewers not to emphasize or interpret the behavior negatively. Speaking on “Fox & Friends,” Kilmeade referenced his own tendency to doze off under certain conditions, attributing such episodes to limited sleep and the demands of a busy schedule. He suggested that similar factors, along with the pressures of the presidency and the president’s age, could explain Trump’s reported moments of apparent sleep during official events.

The discussion comes amid increased public scrutiny of Trump’s physical and cognitive health. Observers have noted visible signs such as bruising on his hands, swelling in his ankles, and a rash on his neck, alongside occasional reports of him appearing to doze off in meetings. Despite these concerns, Kilmeade argued that Trump’s cognitive abilities remain consistent over time, claiming that his speech and responses today are similar to those from decades earlier.

In his remarks, Kilmeade also compared public perceptions of Trump’s health to criticisms previously directed at former President Joe Biden, urging audiences not to equate the two leaders’ cognitive conditions. He emphasized that Trump continues to handle increasingly complex political issues and insisted that concerns about mental decline are overstated. The segment ultimately framed the discussion as a defense of the president’s fitness for office while acknowledging the recurring public debate over his health and stamina.
external.title: Fox News Host Acknowledges Reports of Trump Dozing in Meetings While Defending His Cognitive Health

The Apple rumors were true, once again. This week, the company announced that Tim Cook will be stepping down from his CEO role on September 1. Replacing him will be John Ternus, who currently serves as Apple's SVP of hardware engineering. In this episode, Devindra and Engadget's Nathan Ingraham discuss Cook's legacy as Apple's CEO, and pontificate about how Ternus may change things. We're going from Apple being led by a logistics guru, to being driven by a product and engineering wizard. Surely, that will have some impact on future products.

 Amazon S3 Tables deliver the first cloud object store with built-in Apache Iceberg support, streamlining tabular data storage at scale. S3 Tables automatically perform continual table maintenance to optimize query efficiency and reduce storage costs as your data lake grows and evolves. Because S3 Tables support the Apache Iceberg standard, your data is easily queryable by both AWS and third-party engines. With the Intelligent-Tiering storage class, S3 Tables automatically manage costs based on access patterns with no performance impact or operational overhead.

 For more information about the AWS Regions where S3 Tables are available, see https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/s3-tables-regions-quotas.html#s3-tables-regions.

AWS IoT Core now supports direct messaging for point-to-point communication

https://aws.amazon.com/iot-core/ now supports the ability to send point-to-point messages to any connected device, providing better visibility into message delivery and lower messaging cost. AWS IoT Core is a fully...

#AWS

external.description:
https://aws.amazon.com/iot-core/ now supports the ability to send point-to-point messages to any connected device, providing better visibility into message delivery and lower messaging cost. AWS IoT Core is a fully managed service that securely connects IoT devices to the AWS cloud, and enables bi-directional messaging between IoT devices and cloud services.
 
 Previously, sending messages to a single IoT device required publishing to a topic subscribed by the device, with no built-in way to confirm delivery from the receiving device. With the https://docs.aws.amazon.com/iot/latest/developerguide/direct-messaging.html, you can send a message directly to any device connected to AWS IoT Core, and opt-in to receive delivery acknowledgement from the device. AWS IoT Core also uses the delivery acknowledgement to provide detailed API response codes and emit Amazon CloudWatch Logs, giving you visibility into message delivery status and failure reasons.
 
 Direct messaging is available in all AWS Regions where AWS IoT Core is available, including Amazon China and AWS GovCloud (US). To get started, see the direct messaging https://docs.aws.amazon.com/iot/latest/developerguide/direct-messaging.html. For pricing details, visit the AWS IoT Core https://aws.amazon.com/iot-core/pricing/.
external.title: AWS IoT Core now supports direct messaging for point-to-point communication
